concepts.title: The Zamindari System
concepts.description: The Zamindari System was a feudal landholding system in India during the British colonial period. Under this system, the British government granted land to local lords (Zamindars) who were responsible for collecting taxes from peasants and paying a portion of it to the British. This led to the exploitation of peasants and the concentration of land in the hands of a few wealthy landlords.

concepts.title: The role of the Indian National Congress in the countryside
concepts.description: The Indian National Congress played a significant role in the countryside by organizing peasant movements and agitations against the British colonial policies. The Congress helped to galvanize the peasants and mobilize them against the exploitation by the British and the Zamindars.

concepts.title: The Bengal Famine of 1943
concepts.description: The Bengal Famine of 1943 was a devastating famine that occurred in Bengal during World War II. The famine was caused by a combination of factors including the disruption of food supplies, the hoarding of grains by landlords, and the lack of effective government response. The famine led to the deaths of an estimated 2.5 to 3 million people.

concepts.title: Key Features of the Permanent Settlement of 1793
concepts.description: To remember the key features of the Permanent Settlement of 1793, use the mnemonic: 'I PLEDGE'. I - Income from land to remain fixed, P - Permanent settlement of revenue, L - Landlords to collect revenue from peasants, E - European companies to benefit from the settlement, D - Decline of peasant economy, G - Growth of landlordism, E - Exploitation of peasants.

concepts.title: The impact of colonialism on Indian agriculture
concepts.description: Many students tend to oversimplify the impact of colonialism on Indian agriculture. While colonialism did lead to the introduction of new crops and farming techniques, it also had a devastating impact on Indian agriculture. The colonial policies led to the exploitation of peasants, the concentration of land in the hands of a few landlords, and the decline of traditional Indian farming practices.

concepts.title: The main peasant movements of the 20th century
concepts.description: To remember the main peasant movements of the 20th century, recall the following units: Telangana Movement (1946-1951), Naxalite Movement (1967-1971), and the Kisan Sabhas (1920s-1930s). These movements were significant in galvanizing the peasants and mobilizing them against the exploitation by the British and the Zamindars.

concepts.title: The role of the British colonial government in the countryside
concepts.description: The British colonial government played a significant role in the countryside by implementing policies that exploited the peasants and concentrated land in the hands of a few landlords. The government's policies led to the decline of traditional Indian farming practices, the growth of landlordism, and the exploitation of peasants.

concepts.title: The impact of the Green Revolution on Indian agriculture
concepts.description: The Green Revolution had a significant impact on Indian agriculture, leading to an increase in food production and a decline in poverty. However, it also had negative consequences, including the displacement of small farmers, the concentration of land in the hands of a few large farmers, and the increased use of chemical fertilizers and pesticides.
